title: Sim-to-Real Surgical Robot Learning and Autonomous Planning for Internal Tissue Points Manipulation using Reinforcement Learning
year_basis: Accepted by IEEE RA-L in February 2023; arXiv:2306.14085 v1 appeared 25 Jun 2023
open_source_notes: No paper-specific code release was identified; the simulation environment is built with the open-source SOFA framework.
task_domain: Surgical Robotics; Deformable Tissue Manipulation; Dual-Arm Manipulation
primary_problem: Sim-to-Real Policy Learning; Preoperative Planning; Deformable-Object Control
world_model_detail: N/A — the method uses an explicit FEM simulator for training and optimization rather than learning a predictive environment model.
planning_representation: Continuous grasp-point parameters evaluated through an FEM simulator and learned SAC policy
horizon_basis: Bayesian optimization selects one pair of grasping points before a single internal-tissue positioning episode.
hierarchical_planning: Yes — a preoperative grasp-point planner configures a separate low-level manipulation policy
replanning_online: No — grasp points are optimized before execution
learning_type: Sim-to-Real Deep Reinforcement Learning; Domain Randomization; Bayesian Optimization
pretraining: Yes — the control policy is trained entirely in FEM simulation before real deployment
observation_modality: Stereo vision; controlled tissue-point positions; target positions
goal_representation: Desired Cartesian positions of internal tissue points
action_space: Continuous 2D Cartesian displacement commands for two tissue grasping points
benchmark: Custom SOFA FEM indirect simultaneous positioning environment and real phantom-tissue experiments
robot_platform: Two da Vinci Research Kit Patient Side Manipulators with a stereo camera system
main_contribution: Combines simulation-trained SAC control with Bayesian optimization of surgical grasp points for accurate sim-to-real internal tissue positioning.
novelty: Uses a state-augmented MDP to handle inconsistent simulation timing and reuses the learned policy as the evaluator for preoperative grasp-point planning.
limitations: Assumes a mostly flat, mildly warped tissue surface and visually detectable internal points; local force is not directly minimized, and training and planning remain computationally expensive.
future_work: Add realistic 3D tissue reconstruction and internal-point localization, optimize applied force, extend to breast brachytherapy, automate grasping, and accelerate simulation and optimization with GPU parallelism.
evidence_world_model: FEM supplies a hand-engineered physical simulator; no learned action-conditioned transition model is proposed.
evidence_planning: Bayesian optimization evaluates candidate grasp-point pairs in simulation using the trained policy before real execution.
evidence_horizon: The task is one surgical positioning subtask with fixed grasp points, not a multi-stage long-horizon procedure.
review_flags: Explicit simulator-based planning, but not a learned world model. Count as planning while keeping planning_horizon Short.
source_document: Sim-to-Real Surgical Robot Learning and Autonomous Planning for Internal Tissue Points Manipulation Using Reinforcement .txt

title: RT-1: Robotics Transformer for Real-World Control at Scale
authors: Anthony Brohan; Noah Brown; Justice Carbajal; Yevgen Chebotar; Joseph Dabis; Chelsea Finn; Keerthana Gopalakrishnan; Karol Hausman; et al.
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2212.06817 in Dec 2022; published at RSS 2023
open_source_notes: The authors release model code through the Robotics Transformer repository and provide a public project website.
task_domain: Mobile Manipulation; Generalist Robot Policies; Real-World Control
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Scaling; Generalization; Language-Conditioned Control
world_model_detail: N/A — RT-1 directly maps image history and a language instruction to tokenized robot actions.
horizon_basis: Long-horizon execution is demonstrated when RT-1 is used as the low-level skill policy inside the external SayCan planner, including tasks of up to 50 stages.
hierarchical_planning: No — RT-1 itself is the low-level policy rather than the SayCan planner
replanning_online: Yes — actions are regenerated from a six-image history and language instruction at approximately 3 Hz
learning_type: Large-Scale Multitask Imitation Learning; Cross-Domain Data Absorption
pretraining: Yes — trained on approximately 130,000 real-robot episodes spanning more than 700 tasks
observation_modality: History of six RGB images; natural-language instruction
action_space: Eleven discretized outputs: six end-effector motions, gripper opening, three base motions, and an arm/base/terminate mode
benchmark: More than 700 real-world tasks, 3,000 evaluation trials, office-kitchen generalization, simulation-data absorption, and SayCan long-horizon tasks
robot_platform: Fleet of 13 Everyday Robots mobile manipulators
simulation: Yes — simulation data are incorporated in data-absorption experiments
main_contribution: Introduces an efficient high-capacity transformer policy trained at large real-robot scale and demonstrates strong task, object, environment, and data-source generalization.
novelty: Combines language-conditioned FiLM vision encoding, TokenLearner compression, and a decoder-only transformer to run a 35M-parameter policy in real time.
limitations: Imitation learning cannot exceed demonstrator quality; novel instructions must recombine seen concepts, evaluated environments remain similar to training kitchens, and the manipulation tasks are not highly dexterous.
future_work: Expand to more diverse environments, genuinely novel motions, broader and more dexterous instruction sets, and additional heterogeneous data sources.
evidence_world_model: The transformer directly outputs action tokens and does not predict future states, observations, rewards, or dynamics.
evidence_planning: RT-1 performs low-level control; the external SayCan framework supplies long-horizon skill selection.
evidence_horizon: RT-1 enables SayCan executions of up to 50 stages, so task-level long_horizon is Yes while planning_horizon remains None.
review_flags: Core generalist-policy baseline. Long-horizon capability reflects composition by SayCan, not planning inside RT-1.
source_document: RT-1_ Robotics Transformer for Real-World Control at Scale.txt

title: Robot Learning From Randomized Simulations: A Review
authors: Fabio Muratore; Fabio Ramos; Greg Turk; Wenhao Yu; Michael Gienger; Jan Peters
open_source_notes: The article is open access, but no single companion implementation is associated with the broad review.
uses_world_model: N/A — reviews parameterized simulators and learned models but does not propose one world model
world_model_detail: Reviews randomized physics simulators, parameter distributions, system identification, adaptive randomization, and related robust-learning methods.
uses_planning: N/A — policy search and model-based methods are reviewed among broader sim-to-real approaches
horizon_basis: No single planner or normalized horizon is evaluated.
benchmark: Multiple manipulation, locomotion, navigation, drone, and in-hand sim-to-real studies
main_contribution: Provides a comprehensive mathematical and practical review of domain randomization and learning robot policies from randomized simulations.
novelty: Introduces a domain-randomization taxonomy and connects sim-to-real practice with robust optimization, Bayesian inference, system identification, and policy search.
limitations: The reviewed field suffers from labor-intensive simulator construction, incomplete reporting of randomization choices, costly real-world validation, and uncertain transferability estimates.
future_work: Automate real-to-sim-to-real pipelines, improve adaptive and multi-fidelity randomization, develop stronger transfer metrics, and combine safe real-world adaptation with reusable simulation knowledge.
evidence_world_model: Simulators are parameterized generative models, but this review is not a proposed predictive world-model system.
evidence_planning: The survey covers RL, policy search, model learning, and simulation optimization rather than one planning algorithm.
evidence_horizon: Planning horizon is not a paper-level variable in this survey.
review_flags: Supporting sim-to-real survey; retain for domain-randomization and simulation-method analyses, but exclude from method and world-model counts.
source_document: Robot Learning From Randomized Simulations_ A Review.txt

title: Robotic world models—conceptualization, review, and engineering best practices
authors: Ryo Sakagami; Florian S. Lay; Andreas Dömel; Martin J. Schuster; Alin Albu-Schäffer; Freek Stulp
open_source_notes: The article is open access, but no single codebase accompanies this conceptual and engineering review.
task_domain: General Robotics; Software Architecture; World Modeling
primary_problem: Survey; World-Model Conceptualization; Engineering Best Practices
uses_world_model: N/A — the paper reviews and defines multiple meanings of robotic world models
world_model_family: N/A — classical shared-state architectures and predictive neural world models are both reviewed
world_model_detail: Defines a world model through its boundary, internal state, and operations, emphasizing information shared among sensing, planning, and acting components.
uses_planning: N/A — planners are consumers of shared world-model state in many reviewed systems
planning_representation: Multiple: geometric maps, semantic knowledge, belief state, symbolic representations, and learned latent dynamics
horizon_basis: No single planner or task horizon is evaluated.
benchmark: Fifteen robotic world-model case studies spanning approximately four decades
main_contribution: Proposes consistent terminology, design dimensions, and software-engineering guidelines for implementing robotic world models.
novelty: Frames world-model design through boundary, state, and operations and derives guidance from 'design for use,' DRY, and low-coupling/high-cohesion principles.
limitations: There is no universal world-model design; guidelines can conflict or be infeasible, and appropriate boundaries and abstraction levels depend strongly on the robot, environment, and task.
future_work: Design world models for less controlled environments and increasingly complex systems containing more diverse hardware and software components.
evidence_world_model: The review explicitly contrasts classical shared-information world models with deep predictive dynamics models.
evidence_planning: A world model is treated as a component that consolidates information for multiple planners, sensors, and actors.
evidence_horizon: The review does not report a method-level planning horizon.
review_flags: Definition-sensitive survey: its broad software-architecture meaning includes non-predictive shared state, unlike the master dataset's strict method-level predictive-world-model rule.
source_document: Robotic world models—conceptualization, review, and engineering best practices.txt

title: Review of machine learning methods in soft robotics
authors: Daekyum Kim; Sang-Hun Kim; Taekyoung Kim; Brian Byunghyun Kang; Minhyuk Lee; Wookeun Park; Subyeong Ku; DongWook Kim; Junghan Kwon; Hochang Lee; Joonbum Bae; Yong-Lae Park; Kyu-Jin Cho; Sungho Jo
open_source_notes: The review article is open access and its collected datasets are deposited in OSF under DOI 10.17605/OSF.IO/4MYV7.
task_domain: Soft Robotics; Soft Sensing; Soft Actuation; Manipulation; Wearables
primary_problem: Survey; Machine Learning; Modeling and Control of Soft Robots
human_robot_interaction: N/A — wearable and assistive interaction applications are reviewed
uses_world_model: N/A — the survey includes learned dynamics and model-based control but is not organized around world models
world_model_detail: Reviews supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement-learning approaches for nonlinear sensing, state estimation, dynamics modeling, and control.
uses_planning: N/A — motion planning and model-based control are among many reviewed applications
horizon_basis: No single planning system or normalized horizon is evaluated.
observation_modality: Soft pressure, strain, tactile arrays, vision, proprioception, and wearable sensing
goal_representation: Multiple sensing, calibration, pose-estimation, grasping, and control objectives
action_space: Multiple soft-actuator and manipulator control spaces
benchmark: Multiple soft sensors, pneumatic and cable-driven actuators, grippers, manipulators, and wearable systems
main_contribution: Categorizes machine-learning methods across soft sensors, actuators, grippers, manipulators, and wearable systems and analyzes algorithmic trends.
novelty: Cross-indexes learning algorithms by soft-robot hardware type and task, separating sensing, dynamics modeling, model-based control, and model-free control.
limitations: The field requires large datasets and faces unexpected error sources, nonlinear and time-varying material behavior, limited real-time computation, and relatively little unsupervised learning.
future_work: Develop efficient embedded and real-time learning, better transfer and generalization, multimodal soft sensing, robust lifetime and degradation prediction, and richer simulation-to-hardware validation.
evidence_world_model: Some reviewed studies learn soft-body dynamics, but the article does not propose a unified predictive world model.
evidence_planning: Motion planning and reinforcement learning are reviewed as applications within a much broader soft-robot learning taxonomy.
evidence_horizon: No paper-level planning horizon is defined.
review_flags: Peripheral to the core world-model and long-horizon manipulation corpus; retain for deformable and soft-robot context.
source_document: Review of machine learning methods in soft robotics.txt
